sections.heading: Types of Nouns in Detail
sections.body:
1. Common Nouns: general names (city, person, book)
2. Proper Nouns: specific names, always capitalized (Paris, Ali, Harry Potter)
3. Concrete Nouns: can be sensed (table, music, perfume)
4. Abstract Nouns: ideas/concepts (courage, freedom, knowledge)
5. Collective Nouns: groups (team, family, audience, flock)
6. Compound Nouns: two words combined (toothpaste, mother-in-law, well-being)

Note: A word can be different noun types in different contexts:
• "Love is beautiful" — abstract
• "My love for you is deep" — concrete (person)

sections.heading: Pronoun Types and Rules
sections.body:
Subject pronouns: I, you, he, she, it, we, they — used as the subject
"I go to school." "They play football."

Object pronouns: me, you, him, her, it, us, them — used as the object
"She called me." "We helped them."

Possessive pronouns: mine, yours, his, hers, its, ours, theirs
"This book is mine." "The choice is theirs."

Reflexive pronouns: myself, yourself, himself, herself, itself, ourselves, themselves
"I hurt myself." "They enjoyed themselves."

Common mistakes:
• "Me and Ali went" → "Ali and I went" (subject position)
• "Give it to he" → "Give it to him" (object position)

sections.heading: Possessives: apostrophe usage
sections.body:
Singular possession: add 's
"The teacher's book" = one teacher
"A child's toy" = one child

Plural possession: add just '
"The teachers' books" = multiple teachers
"The children's toys" = irregular plural

Irregular: "The men's room" not "mens' room"

After s: "The boss's office" or "The boss' office" (both accepted)

Never use: "its'" (its is already possessive)

sections.heading: Practice
sections.body:
Identify and correct:
1. "Me and him is going to store."
2. "The dogs tail is wagging."
3. "She gave he a book."
4. "A apple a day keeps the doctor away."
5. " informations are important"

Answers:
1. "He and I are going to the store."
2. "The dog's tail is wagging."
3. "She gave him a book."
4. "An apple a day keeps the doctor away."
5. "Information is important" (uncountable — no plural)

summary: Nouns and pronouns form the backbone of English sentences. Pay special attention to: articles (a/an/the), possessive apostrophes, and choosing the right pronoun form.
